item.title: New Mossad director met with CIA chief on Iran
item.description:
The new director of Israel's Mossad spy agency, Roman Gofman, visited Washington two weeks ago for talks about the war in Iran and the Iranian nuclear program, according to two sources with knowledge.

Why it matters: Gofman is one of the closest advisers to Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. It was Gofman's first visit to Washington since he assumed office in June.

Driving the news: The Mossad chief's visit took place right before tensions around the Strait of Hormuz escalated and fighting resumed.

  • One purpose of Gofman's visit was to coordinate with the White House on negotiations with Iran on a nuclear deal, an Israeli source said.

Behind the scenes: The sources said Gofman met with CIA director John Ratcliffe as well as White House officials.

  • Inside Trump's inner circle, Ratcliffe was one of the more skeptical voices on the memorandum of understanding with Iran.
  • He warned before the MOU was signed that Iran would interpret the deal differently than the U.S., including provisions involving the strait.
  • The CIA and the Mossad declined to comment.

item.title: "No one cares": The DSA primary challenge House Democrats are happy to ignore
item.description:
Don't expect a lot of public lamentations from House Democrats next month if Rep. Shri Thanedar (D-Mich.) loses to his Democratic Socialists of America (DSA)-backed primary opponent.

Why it matters: That would be a drastic deviation from recent DSA primary wins, which set off alarm bells among the Democratic establishment.

  • In the telling of both progressive and moderate House Democrats, Thanedar's anomalous political profile and the makeup of his district will make it difficult to extrapolate from the results of his race.
  • "It's somewhat of a unique circumstance," Rep. Greg Meeks (D-N.Y.), a top ally of House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.), told Axios.
  • Another House Democrat, granted anonymity to offer a blunter assessment of how members are viewing the race, told Axios: "No one cares."

State of play: Thanedar will face off with state Rep. Donavan McKinney in an Aug. 4 primary that has been the toughest of his career.

  • Thanedar is without the main advantage that helped him win his predominantly Black, Detroit-based seat in the last two cycles: A divided Democratic primary field of Black opponents.
  • Progressives are also putting their backs into unseating Thanedar this cycle, with Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), DSA, Indivisible, the Working Families Party and Justice Democrats all endorsing McKinney.
  • Thanedar, a wealthy businessman who largely self-funds his campaigns, is backed by AIPAC, House Democratic leadership and both the Congressional Progressive Caucus and center-left New Democrat Coalition.

Between the lines: Unlike in New York, Colorado and elsewhere, House Democrats aren't looking at this race as a watershed fight between the DSA and Democratic establishment with national implications.

  • "You're talking about Detroit," said Meeks, noting that the city "has a large African American population" but no Black members of Congress.
  • Another senior House Democrat offered a similar assessment, telling Axios: "This [race] has so many nuances because of the cultural issues in Detroit ... I don't think it's indicative of anything larger."

That's an assessment both ideological wings of the caucus also agree on:

  • Said a senior Congressional Progressive Caucus member: "I really think it's about a very strong Black candidate representing a Black district and votes the current congressman's constituents are upset with."
  • A top New Democrats member told Axios: "Of course DSA is going to claim victory — that has nothing to do with that district."

What we're hearing: Thanedar's oddball political persona and rogue efforts to impeach President Trump have made him something of an outcast figure in the caucus.

  • "I don't think anybody would miss him at all," said a fifth House Democrat who spoke to Axios on the condition of anonymity.
  • A sixth lawmaker fumed that Thanedar "f***ing screwed us" by defying pleas from both leadership and rank-and-file members not to pursue impeachment, with several others saying he is not a "team player."
  • While Thanedar has forged some relationships, colleagues largely characterized him as reserved, isolated and eccentric. When asked about him, multiple House Democrats replied facetiously: "Who?"

The other side: Thanedar told Axios he has "not put a huge emphasis" on endorsements from colleagues and "only recently approached members" but is "proud of the endorsements I have."

  • He pointed to endorsements from more than a dozen colleagues including Jeffries and several other members of Democratic leadership, adding that "more members are expected to endorse us soon."
  • The Detroit congressman also said he "did not run for Congress to be popular on the Hill," telling Axios his "main focus has been, and will continue to be with constituents in my district."
  • "Any of my colleagues are more than welcome to walk around my district with me and see firsthand the love my constituents have for me, and the love I have for them," he added.

Between the lines: Jeffries and his deputies endorsing Thanedar this week was in keeping with a leadership policy of supporting incumbents in all but the most extraordinary cases.

  • A second senior House Democrat, asked about the endorsement, told Axios that leadership will generally stick by any member as long as they are "doing what they need to be doing and keeping their nose clean."
  • The same principle applies to endorsements from ideological caucuses: "We support our incumbents," the top New Dems member told Axios.

The bottom line: "No one is staying up at night worrying about him," said the first House Democrat who spoke to Axios anonymously.

media:content.media:description: Rep. Shri Thanedar attends a House Homeland Security Committee hearing at the U.S. Capitol on Feb. 10. Photo: Samuel Corum/Getty Images

item.title: Exclusive: Nvidia's Jensen Huang defends Chinese AI amid Kimi panic
item.description:
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang believes America has nothing to fear from China's open-source AI models — and everything to fear from the growing campaign to ban them.

  • "These Chinese models are excellent," Huang told Axios' Mike Allen on Tuesday in an exclusive interview for our "Behind the Curtain" video series. "Open-source models that are excellent should be used."

Why it matters: The world's top AI chipmaker says American companies should "absolutely" be allowed to use Chinese models — a direct challenge to Trump officials and the U.S. labs lobbying Washington to shut them out.

Among them are Nvidia's own customers, OpenAI and Anthropic, which have accused Chinese rivals of siphoning capabilities from their models and warned that Beijing's open-source surge threatens America's AI lead.

  • Huang rejected the idea that OpenAI and Anthropic should be afraid of open models, arguing they expand the market by giving more people a first taste of AI, while many users will still pay for the convenience, reliability and stronger performance of closed services.
  • "There's no scenario where China runs U.S. companies off the road," Huang told us. "Zero possibility."
  • Huang sat down with Axios in Fort Worth, Texas, at the opening of a new phase of an advanced manufacturing plant where Taiwan-based Wistron makes AI infrastructure for Nvidia, showing the U.S. is capable of some of the world's most sophisticated manufacturing.

The big picture: The release of Kimi K3 by Beijing-based Moonshot AI has triggered the most intense bout of AI panic since DeepSeek rattled financial markets in January 2025.

  • Kimi combines three qualities rarely seen together: near-frontier performance, dramatically lower prices and open weights that developers will soon be able to download and customize.
  • That combination helped fuel a sell-off of Nvidia and other chip stocks, reviving fears that cheaper, more efficient models could undercut the case for the industry's massive AI infrastructure buildout.

Zoom in: Huang says Wall Street and Washington have the Kimi shock exactly backward.

  • "The market misunderstood the impact of DeepSeek the first time," he told Axios, adding that Wall Street has "misunderstood the impact of Kimi again this time."
  • His argument is simple: Cheaper, open models will bring AI to more people and businesses, increasing demand for the chips, data centers and computing power Nvidia sells.
  • "Free AI should be great for hardware," Huang said. "Free AI should be great for chips. Free AI should be great for data centers."

Zoom out: When it comes to policy, Huang believes restricting open models in the name of national security could leave America more vulnerable.

  • He rejected the "misconception" that downloaded Chinese models create a "backdoor" to Beijing, arguing that companies can customize them and control their access inside secure "sandboxes."
  • Huang suggested openness makes AI more secure, not less, because outside researchers can inspect the models, expose weaknesses and build defenses.
  • "If everything just becomes one single model, one single point of attack, one single source of failure, I think the world is much, much more vulnerable," he said.

Huang applied the same logic to American models, and called for Anthropic to make its restricted cyber model, Claude Mythos, available to "everyone" rather than limiting access.

  • Huang argued that companies should harden powerful models through testing and rapid fixes rather than restricting access from the outset. "Mythos should be available as a service," he said. "Remember: Just because Mythos is not available, open models are available anyhow. So I think: Let Anthropic run."
  • "Holding Anthropic back is not in the benefit of the United States," he said.

Between the lines: Huang sees the AI race itself very differently from Washington, where some Trump advisers have cast China's gains as a five-alarm threat to U.S. dominance.

  • He dismissed the notion that it's "a race with an endpoint": "We're going to continue to use AI forever. The United States is going to be here for a long time. China's going to be here for a long time."

The intrigue: Huang's comments came hours after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Fox Business that the administration is examining Chinese AI models for stolen U.S. intellectual property — and considering sanctions in response.

  • "If we see ... that overseas models are stealing from our great companies, we have the ability to sanction them because of this theft," Bessent said, citing "watermarks" of U.S. models inside Chinese ones.

Huang drew a different conclusion. "Distillation, learning from AI, learning from other sources of knowledge, is fundamental to intelligence," he told Axios.

  • He said companies should still face consequences for violating privacy or contracts, but his broader message was clear: Target the misconduct, not the models.

item.title: Scoop: "Regime Change" sells 500,000 — on track for nonfiction bestseller of '26
item.description:
"Regime Change," the juicy exposé on President Trump's second term by Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan, has passed the publishing milestone of 500,000 units sold (hardcover, audio, e-book) in just one month.

  • Publishing sources tell me the blockbuster is on track to be the bestselling nonfiction book of 2026. Simon & Schuster has ordered a seventh printing, bringing the total hardcovers in print to 460,000.
  • "Reports of the death of the nonfiction book have been greatly exaggerated," said Matt Latimer of the Javelin agency, which represents Haberman.

But this is a rare feat. "This doesn't necessarily mean that Trump books are back," said Keith Urbahn, Latimer's co-founder at Javelin. "It's still uphill sledding for political books."

  • "This book is different," he added. "Maggie and Swan pierced the veil of a White House that has been pretty locked down on scoops and scenes. 'Regime Change' is a unicorn of publishing. It goes well beyond political circles to people who are hungry for a narrative and frame to make sense of Trump 2.0. Even Trump and his team aren't disputing the facts."

Zoom in: The book has been No. 1 on the New York Times nonfiction bestseller list for three weeks running.

  • "Regime Change" has also been No. 1 in Canada, Australia and the Netherlands, and No. 2 in the UK.

The big picture: Just two months ago, a Wall Street Journal article declared that "Dad Books" — including books about politics and current affairs, as well as biographies and histories — "are a dying breed."

  • The proximate causes: audiobooks, Substack newsletters, Netflix documentaries, YouTube videos and podcasts.

Upcoming author appearances.

item.title: Trump, Rubio don't plan to stop Tate brothers' extradition to the U.K.
item.description:
President Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio aren't planning to block the extradition to the U.K. of the Tate brothers, the notorious "manosphere" influencers accused of rape and human trafficking. Why it matters: Andrew and Tristan Tate were part of the coalition of hyper-masculine social media voices advocating for Trump when he was reelected in 2024. The brothers have reported connections to two of Trump's sons and have had support from top Trump advisers.

  • Officially and publicly, Trump and his administration have kept the Tates at arm's length because of their sordid reputations.
  • "The U.S. has an extradition treaty with the U.K. and the administration intends to honor it in this case," a senior administration official told Axios.

Driving the news: U.S. marshals arrested the Tates in Miami on Saturday after British authorities issued 38 new sex-crime charges against the brothers, bringing the total combined counts against them to 59.

  • They also were indicted on separate sex-crime charges in Romania in 2023.

Zoom in: In court filings, the Justice Department made clear that Rubio has "sole discretion" to block their extradition to the U.K. if he believes the brothers are being politically persecuted.

  • On Monday, Tate attorney Joe McBride told reporters outside the federal courthouse in Miami that the charges against the two are "politically motivated. There's no question about it."
  • McBride said the brothers are innocent and will fight extradition.
  • A senior State Department official told Axios that "the secretary has not spoken to the Tate Brothers or their lawyers. The State Department has no plans to act on this matter at this time."

Behind the scenes: Those who know Rubio's thinking say the secretary, a conservative Catholic who fought as a U.S. senator for stronger human trafficking laws and protections for victims, isn't inclined to help the Tates.

  • "Marco thinks these guys are scum," said one source. "Now if the president overrules him, which I don't think is likely, he's got a problem."
  • A source familiar with Trump's thinking said the president wants nothing to do with the brothers.
  • "The Tates want to pretend they were responsible for the president's victory [in 2024], but they weren't part of our strategy and the president doesn't know them," a former Trump campaign adviser said.

Flashback: After Andrew Tate first faced rape allegations in the U.K. in 2015 the brothers moved to Romania, where they began running their online porn business, according to a damning long-form investigation by the New Yorker called "Andrew Tate's Empire of Abuse."

  • When Trump won his first election in 2016, Andrew Tate posted supportive MAGA tweets and began messaging the president's oldest son, Donald Trump Jr.
  • Tate's online profile grew amid a masculine backlash to the 2018 #MeToo movement. His online courses sought to teach young men how to profit from turning women into "sex slaves," and awarded "PhD's" that stood for "Pimpin' Hos Degree."

As criminal investigations into the Tates grew, the brothers drew a parallel with the "lawfare" that Trump said he faced while running for reelection in 2023. During that time, Andrew Tate sat for softball interviews with podcasters Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens. Trump gave a Truth Social shoutout to Tristan Tate.

  • After an assassination attempt on Trump in 2024, Andrew Tate told reporters, "I'm very close with the Trump family. I know them well." He said he spoke with Trump's youngest son, Barron, and called the president "a bulletproof badass."

The intrigue: A month after Trump returned to office last year, Romanian authorities reinstated the Tate brothers' right to travel outside of the country as prosecutors reexamined procedural issues with their case.

  • Romania's foreign minister denied that the decision was linked to pressure from a Trump adviser, Ric Grenell, who had spoken favorably of the Tates. Grenell told Axios the minister broached the topic of the Tates with him and he made no demands.
  • Shortly afterward, the Tates flew from Romania on a private plane to Fort Lauderdale, Fla., where Customs and Border Patrol officers seized their phones.
  • The Tates got their phones back, however, thanks to the intervention of their former lawyer, Paul Ingrassia, who then was a White House liaison to the Department of Homeland Security, according to Pro Publica. Ingrassia told Axios the report was inaccurate.

Inside the room: Last week the Tates were in D.C., where they met with some members of Congress and rubbed elbows with insiders at the exclusive club, The Ned. There, they ran into Trump confidant and special envoy Paulo Zampolli, the Daily Mail first reported.

  • Zampolli invited the Tates to join 150 guests at his July 15 "summer soiree" at Zampolli's Dupont Circle home.
  • Zampolli told Axios he'd met the Tates three times before, starting at the Milken Institute Global Conference this year in Los Angeles, where he sat for a friendly interview on Andrew Tate's podcast.
  • "I did not know these allegations about them," Zampolli said. "That is on me."

What they're saying: One attendee at Zampolli's party said it was attended by people from the Justice, State and Treasury departments, as well as D.C. insiders and even some World Cup soccer players.

  • "The Tate brothers were like celebrities. People wanted to take pictures with them," the source said. "You never would've known they were about to get arrested."

media:content.media:description: Andrew Tate (r) and his brother Tristan speak to reporters in Bucharest last year. Photo: Daniel Mihailescu/AFP via Getty Images

item.title: U.S. measles cases surpass last year's total: Tracker
item.description:
The U.S. measles case count for this year has surged past last year's total and now stands at 2,295 — a level that hasn't been seen in 35 years, according to a Johns Hopkins University tracker that compiles state and local health data.

Why it matters: It's the latest sign that the virus is rebounding in areas with low vaccine coverage, threatening the United States' measles elimination status, which is due to be reviewed in November.

The big picture: The majority of cases are in unvaccinated people. While overall measles vaccination rates are still relatively high, public health officials say they must remain above 95% to maintain herd immunity.

By the numbers: As of Tuesday, there were nine more cases than in all of 2025, per the Johns Hopkins U.S. measles tracker.

  • Jurisdictions including South Carolina, Utah, Texas, Florida and Arizona have the most reported cases.
  • The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reported 2,260 confirmed cases in its last update on July 16. The agency said there were 34 new outbreaks reported this year.
  • The last time measles cases were higher was in 1991, when the U.S. had 9,643 cases, according to CDC data.

Threat level: Measles symptoms include a cough, high fever and conjunctivitis as well as a telltale rash, but can escalate into complications such as severe ear infections, pneumonia and encephalitis or inflammation of the brain that can lead to permanent brain damage and even death, per Johns Hopkins Medicine.

  • On average, measles kills between 1 and 3 of every 1,000 infected children.

The rebound comes at a time when a number of key top government health positions — including CDC director and surgeon general — remain vacant, and after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. initially downplayed the virus' threat.

  • He's since said the measles, mumps and rubella (MMR) vaccine is the most effective way to prevent measles spread while noting vaccination is a personal choice.

The rebound comes at a time when a number of key top government health positions — including CDC director and surgeon general — remain vacant, and after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. initially downplayed the virus' threat.

item.title: Senate rabble-rousers are gaining power
item.description:
Hardline conservatives are gaining power in the Senate atop key committees.

Why it matters: The senators known for rankling GOP leadership now have gavels that let them hold hearings, advance legislation and even issue subpoenas.

Driving the news: Sen. Ron Johnson (R-Wis.) will be the new chair of the Budget Committee, Senate Republicans agreed in their closed-door lunch Tuesday.

  • The committee, which has a key role in reconciliation, had been chaired by the late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.)
  • Johnson also chairs the Homeland Security subcommittee on investigations, allowing him to launch probes into everything from former President Biden's health to Meta's alleged cooperation with China.

What to watch: Sen. Mike Lee (R-Utah) is next in line to take the gavel on the powerful Judiciary Committee next Congress, following Graham's death and Sen. John Cornyn's (R-Texas) primary loss.

  • Lee would have to forfeit his current Energy Committee chair post.
  • He indicated to the Washington Examiner he would be willing to do so.

Zoom out: Fiscal hawk Rand Paul (R-Ky.) chairs the Homeland Security Committee, where he has been skeptical of massive agency budgets.

  • He recently voted "no" on Markwayne Mullin's nomination to be DHS secretary.
  • Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.) chairs the Aging Committee, and he also leads the GOP steering committee, an unofficial, conservative group.
  • Scott has leveraged the group's weekly lunch to bring in top government officials, including President Trump, to discuss current issues.

The bottom line: The Senate runs on seniority. That means even senators willing to buck their party or criticize leadership can climb committee ranks.

item.title: Scoop: Progressives scorch Gillibrand over crypto
item.description:
Progressive groups are opening a vicious new front in the fight over crypto legislation, targeting Sen Kirsten Gillibrand as she tries to broker a compromise on the CLARITY Act, which has unresolved issues on its ethics provisions.

Why it matters: The blistering — and deeply personal — attack from Indivisible, Demand Progress and the Revolving Door Project complicates already delicate negotiations.

  • It's also a warning shot to Democrats: Progressive groups are prepared to make support for the bill politically painful, even if it means another bruising intraparty fight over one of the crypto industry's top legislative priorities.
  • "Senator Kirsten Gillibrand, chair of the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee (DSCC), is a prime example of a Democratic leader whose conduct undermines efforts to hold the Trump administration accountable for their rampant corruption," the groups wrote in a letter sent to every Democratic Senate office Tuesday evening.
  • "Fair or not, criticisms of the Trump administration's overwhelming corruption fall flat when voters perceive the critics to be engaged in substantively similar behavior."

The groups argue that Gillibrand's son's involvement in an equity trading platform leaves Democrats vulnerable to the very charge they are trying to level against President Trump.

  • Trump's crypto ventures have generated an estimated $600 million windfall, testing long-standing norms around presidents profiting from businesses affected by government decisions.

The other side: Gillibrand has called for Congress to prevent all elected officials, and their spouses, from issuing or sponsoring their own digital assets.

  • "This is a commonsense requirement that should get broad bipartisan support — public officials and their spouses should not be issuing memecoins," she said in a June statement.

State of play: Finding 60 votes for the CLARITY Act this summer is a high-wire act, but passing the bill remains a top priority for Senate Republicans, the White House and the crypto industry.

  • Sens. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio) and Cynthia Lummis (R-Wyo.) are working with the White House on compromise language for the ethics provisions. They appear to be making progress, though the details of the compromise haven't been released.
  • In addition to the talks with the White House, bipartisan talks are underway, with Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) leading Republicans in negotiating the ethics language.

The intrigue: Looming over the negotiations is the crypto industry's political war chest. Fairshake, its flagship super PAC, has some $125 million on hand.

  • The industry's political muscle gives both parties another incentive to reach a deal — and another reason for progressives to dig in.
  • Republicans are eager to put that money to work to help defend their Senate majority, but — as with the legislative negotiations — the clock is ticking for those dollars to have maximum impact in the midterm elections.

What we're hearing: Both parties view the ethics language as the biggest remaining obstacle to securing the 60 votes needed to pass the CLARITY Act.

  • The bill cleared the Senate Banking Committee in May with support from two Democrats, Sens. Ruben Gallego (Ariz.) and Angela Alsobrooks (Md.), underscoring the bill's bipartisan potential and the difficult coalition it will take to reach the Senate floor.

Zoom out: Crypto regulation continues to divide Democrats on both policy and politics.

  • Last year's passage of the GENIUS Act — which established the first federal regulatory framework for dollar-backed stablecoins — sparked tense internal Democratic debates over both the merits of the legislation and Trump's financial ties to the crypto industry.
  • In the end, 18 Senate Democrats voted for the bill.

Editor's note: This story has been updated to clarify that Gillibrand's son's startup -- The American Perpetuals Exchange Corp. -- is not directly involved in the crypto industry.

item.title: World Cup final draws record 62.8M U.S. viewers
item.description:
Sunday's FIFA World Cup final drew more than 62.8 million viewers in the U.S., making it the most-watched World Cup final and soccer telecast in American history.

Why it matters: The record audience underscores soccer's growing popularity in the U.S. and is likely to boost the value of future media rights.

By the numbers: The game, which saw Spain beat defending champion Argentina 1–0 in extra time, also was the most-watched soccer match in Spanish-language media history, Telemundo said.

  • Nearly 39 million U.S. viewers watched the game on Fox, which had exclusive English-language broadcast rights in the U.S. Those figures are up 132% from the 2022 final on Fox, the company said.
  • Another nearly 24 million U.S. viewers tuned into the broadcast on Telemundo, which had exclusive Spanish-language broadcast rights in the U.S. Those figures are up 166% from the 2022 final on Telemundo.
Data: Nielsen; Note: Includes English and Spanish broadcasts. Nielsen began including out-of-home and streaming viewing in its official national TV ratings in 2020; Chart: Kerry Flynn/Axios

Streaming played a big role in the tournament's reach. June was the biggest subscriber acquisition month for Fox's streaming service, Fox One, according to Antenna.

  • Fox One added an estimated 2.8 million new subscribers in June, more than 2.5 times its previous monthly record in January during the NFL playoffs, per Antenna.
  • Nearly all of Fox's new June subscribers — 93% — were first-time customers rather than returning users.

The intrigue: Peacock, which streams Telemundo, also saw strong subscriber growth, recording an estimated 3.75 million U.S. sign-ups in June, per Antenna.

  • Streaming accounted for nearly half of Telemundo's World Cup audience, up from 30% in 2022, per Telemundo.
  • Telemundo's coverage resonated beyond Spanish speakers, as some preferred the energetic commentary, lack of commercials during the hydration breaks and cheaper cost of Peacock compared to Fox One.

Between the lines: America hosting the tournament, a competitive U.S. men's national team and broader streaming distribution helped push viewership to new heights in the U.S.

  • The U.S. team's matches set audience records. Fox said the U.S. opening match against Paraguay on June 12 became the most-watched FIFA Men's World Cup telecast in English-language U.S. history, averaging 18 million viewers.
  • The U.S. men's victory over Bosnia and Herzegovina on July 1 set the record as the biggest English-language TV audience for any soccer match in America with 26.4 million viewers on Fox.
  • The U.S. team's loss to Belgium on July 6 later bested the record with 37.2 million viewers on Fox, per Nielsen, along with 12.9 million viewers on Telemundo, for more than 50 million combined.
  • The July 5 match between Mexico and England came close to that record with 46.7 million viewers on Fox and Telemundo.

The big picture: The FIFA World Cup has historically attracted the largest global audience of any recurring television event worldwide.

  • FIFA has not yet released worldwide figures. The 2022 World Cup final between Argentina and France held the previous final game viewership record at 1.42 billion worldwide, per FIFA.
  • Live sports remain one of the few programming categories capable of attracting massive live audiences, driving both advertising revenue and streaming subscriptions.
  • FIFA also experimented with bringing more American-style entertainment to the event, including its first halftime show for a World Cup final, to help the tournament appeal beyond traditional soccer fans.

What to watch: FIFA is expected to cash in on the tournament's success by opening up the media rights bidding process for the 2030 World Cup.

  • Fox and Telemundo's current agreements ran through the 2026 tournament, after FIFA extended the deals without a competitive bidding process for the 2022 tournament.
  • FIFA has reportedly alerted media companies that English- and Spanish-language U.S. rights are likely to be sold together as a package for 2030, per CNBC, with Netflix, Disney and YouTube all interested in bidding.

item.title: Scoop: Trump's top MAGA advisers gather to plot midterms
item.description:
President Trump's top political advisers inside and outside of the White House are meeting Tuesday afternoon to review polling, their messaging and strategy ahead of the Nov. 3 midterms.

Why it matters: Trump's super PAC and the Republican National Committee are sitting on about $530 million, and now his team is figuring out how, when and where to deploy it.

  • That doesn't include what insiders say is hundreds of millions of dollars more in Trump's dark-money political nonprofit, Securing American Greatness.

Zoom in: The meeting at the Four Seasons hotel in D.C. is the second of what are becoming regular Trump team conclaves to coordinate the GOP, its candidates and his political operation. Trump won't be there, but these top MAGA shot-callers will be:

  • Tony Fabrizio, Trump's longtime top pollster, who will give a presentation about the midterm picture.
  • James Blair, Trump's hands-on political operations chief in charge of executing a midterm plan.
  • Susie Wiles, White House chief of staff and Trump's longest-serving political adviser.
  • Chris LaCivita, Trump's former campaign co-manager with Wiles.
  • Joe Gruters, RNC chair under Trump and a longtime ally of Wiles.
  • Cabinet officials (except Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who's in Manila on official business).

Zoom out: Republicans acknowledge it will be tough to maintain control of the U.S. House with Trump's poll numbers bottoming out amid rising gas prices and the war in Iran.

  • But Trump's political operatives say voters are wary of Democrats' progressive overreach, and they think this election will be about who Americans hate more, not who they love.
  • Republicans also sparked an unprecedented round of mid-decade redistricting that could net the GOP 10 additional House seats, making that chamber easier to defend.

Yes, but: Trump is fighting political gravity. With few exceptions, a president whose party controls both chambers of Congress usually loses control of at least one in a midterm election.

  • Polls show voters don't believe the economy is in good shape and are blaming Trump.
  • "Voters feel that Trump's economic policies have made things worse — because they have," John Anzalone, a top Democratic pollster who also surveys for the Wall Street Journal, wrote last week on X.

What they're saying: "The Trump tax cuts have saved people real money and the president is keeping Americans safe," said one Trump operative involved in Tuesday's meeting.

  • "We have the manpower, the message and the money to make that clear."

item.title: Scoop: Trump's Cabinet members are joining TikTok after DOJ shift
item.description:
Most of President Trump's Cabinet will launch TikTok accounts Tuesday, a move that comes days after the Justice Department told federal employees it was safe to use the app because of its sale to a U.S.-approved owner.

Why it matters: The move is a striking shift from Trump's first term, when his administration argued that TikTok posed a national security threat because of its ownership by China's ByteDance.

  • By joining the app themselves, Cabinet officials are effectively signaling that TikTok is now an acceptable venue for government messaging.

The background: The 2022 No TikTok on Government Devices Act said TikTok couldn't be used on government-issued phones.

  • But Trump's DOJ now says that law is no longer applicable, given the change in the company's ownership to a U.S.-controlled venture earlier year.

Zoom in: Trump's administration now views TikTok as a powerful tool for reaching young voters.

  • Trump himself has been on the app since 2024, and some of his posts — including one documenting his appearance at a UFC event — have gotten hundreds of millions of views.

item.dc:creator: Alex Isenstadt

item.title: Mike Johnson backs ban on lawmakers dating Hill staff
item.description:
House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) told Axios Tuesday he supports banning members of Congress from having romantic or sexual relationships with Capitol Hill staff, adding momentum to a discussion about tightening House workplace rules.

Why it matters: Congress has been rocked this year by a series of sexual misconduct scandals, prompting bipartisan conversations about reforming how the House deals with sexual harassment and misconduct.

  • "I think it's inappropriate, as it would be in any workplace," Johnson told Axios when asked about lawmakers engaging in relationships with staffers.
  • "We should, at a bare minimum in Congress, have, you know, rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America, so that's an easy one for me," Johnson continued.
  • House rules prohibit members from engaging in sexual relationships with members of their own staff, but do not prohibit relationships with staff members outside their offices.

Driving the news: A bipartisan House task force on sexual misconduct is weighing whether to recommend banning lawmaker-staffer relations.

  • "If you're a member, you really should not be doing that because you have extraordinary power and authority over that individual's career, even if they are not in your office," Rep. Kat Cammack (R-Fla.), the GOP lead on the task force, told Axios Tuesday.
  • "There is a massive power dynamic imbalance," Cammack added.

"From the beginning, we've pushed to extend the prohibition against members having sexual relationships to ALL staff," the task force's Democratic lead, Rep. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.), said in a statement to Axios.

  • "The power dynamic that leads to abuse exists for all staffers, not just those within the members own office," she continued.

Cammack said banning relationships with staff outside of a member's office remains under discussion, though lawmakers have a "difference of opinion" on the proposal.

  • She said the task force is "70% of the way there" on a broader package of recommendations.

Catch up quick: Calls for tougher rules ramped up after two lawmakers — Reps. Tony Gonzales (R-Texas) and Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) — resigned earlier this year amid allegations of sexual misconduct. Swalwell also faces allegations of sexual assault.

  • Swalwell has denied the allegations, and Gonzales admitted to an affair with a staffer who later died by suicide.
  • The Ethics panel lost jurisdiction over the matters when each of the lawmakers stepped down. Both were staring down imminent expulsion votes.

Separately, Axios has reported that Rep. Chuck Edwards (R-N.C.) had singled out two young female staffers for special attention that sources described as inappropriate.

  • Edwards is under House Ethics investigation for alleged sexual harassment and creating a hostile work environment.
  • Rep. Cory Mills (R-Fla.) is also under House Ethics investigation for allegations that center on domestic abuse, stolen valor and financial misconduct, all of which he denies.

Zoom in: The New York Post reported that Senator Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) engaged in romantic relationships with two staffers outside of his office while he served in the House. Asked about the allegations, Gallego told NBC News that he was "not going to engage in gossip."

  • In response, House Conference Chairwoman Lisa McClain (R-Mich.), the highest-ranking woman in GOP leadership, called for a ban on member relations with congressional staff.
  • "Even if a relationship is consensual, there is an inherent power imbalance that can undermine trust and create an appearance of impropriety," McClain wrote on X.

"It's hard to paint with too broad of a brush, but I would support limiting that, given the imbalances and so forth," Rep. Brad Knott (R-N.C.), who sits on the Ethics Committee, told Axios of banning staffer-lawmaker relationships.

  • "You have had members that got engaged and married to staff. That's a little different, you know, but in today's climate, it's probably wiser to do that."

Editor's note: This story has been updated with comments from Reps. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.) and Brad Knott (R-N.C.).
item.content:encoded:
House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) told Axios Tuesday he supports banning members of Congress from having romantic or sexual relationships with Capitol Hill staff, adding momentum to a discussion about tightening House workplace rules.

Why it matters: Congress has been rocked this year by a series of sexual misconduct scandals, prompting bipartisan conversations about reforming how the House deals with sexual harassment and misconduct.

  • "I think it's inappropriate, as it would be in any workplace," Johnson told Axios when asked about lawmakers engaging in relationships with staffers.
  • "We should, at a bare minimum in Congress, have, you know, rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America, so that's an easy one for me," Johnson continued.
  • House rules prohibit members from engaging in sexual relationships with members of their own staff, but do not prohibit relationships with staff members outside their offices.

Driving the news: A bipartisan House task force on sexual misconduct is weighing whether to recommend banning lawmaker-staffer relations.

  • "If you're a member, you really should not be doing that because you have extraordinary power and authority over that individual's career, even if they are not in your office," Rep. Kat Cammack (R-Fla.), the GOP lead on the task force, told Axios Tuesday.
  • "There is a massive power dynamic imbalance," Cammack added.

"From the beginning, we've pushed to extend the prohibition against members having sexual relationships to ALL staff," the task force's Democratic lead, Rep. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.), said in a statement to Axios.

  • "The power dynamic that leads to abuse exists for all staffers, not just those within the members own office," she continued.

Cammack said banning relationships with staff outside of a member's office remains under discussion, though lawmakers have a "difference of opinion" on the proposal.

  • She said the task force is "70% of the way there" on a broader package of recommendations.

Catch up quick: Calls for tougher rules ramped up after two lawmakers — Reps. Tony Gonzales (R-Texas) and Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) — resigned earlier this year amid allegations of sexual misconduct. Swalwell also faces allegations of sexual assault.

  • Swalwell has denied the allegations, and Gonzales admitted to an affair with a staffer who later died by suicide.
  • The Ethics panel lost jurisdiction over the matters when each of the lawmakers stepped down. Both were staring down imminent expulsion votes.

Separately, Axios has reported that Rep. Chuck Edwards (R-N.C.) had singled out two young female staffers for special attention that sources described as inappropriate.

  • Edwards is under House Ethics investigation for alleged sexual harassment and creating a hostile work environment.
  • Rep. Cory Mills (R-Fla.) is also under House Ethics investigation for allegations that center on domestic abuse, stolen valor and financial misconduct, all of which he denies.

Zoom in: The New York Post reported that Senator Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) engaged in romantic relationships with two staffers outside of his office while he served in the House. Asked about the allegations, Gallego told NBC News that he was "not going to engage in gossip."

  • In response, House Conference Chairwoman Lisa McClain (R-Mich.), the highest-ranking woman in GOP leadership, called for a ban on member relations with congressional staff.
  • "Even if a relationship is consensual, there is an inherent power imbalance that can undermine trust and create an appearance of impropriety," McClain wrote on X.

"It's hard to paint with too broad of a brush, but I would support limiting that, given the imbalances and so forth," Rep. Brad Knott (R-N.C.), who sits on the Ethics Committee, told Axios of banning staffer-lawmaker relationships.

  • "You have had members that got engaged and married to staff. That's a little different, you know, but in today's climate, it's probably wiser to do that."

Editor's note: This story has been updated with comments from Reps. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.) and Brad Knott (R-N.C.).

item.title: Hugging Face breach: OpenAI claims its models were responsible
item.description:
OpenAI said Tuesday that models it was testing escaped their sandbox and compromised parts of AI platform Hugging Face's production infrastructure last week.

Why it matters: It is the latest sign that capable AI models can pose serious cybersecurity risks even when they're being tested for defensive or research purposes.

Catch up quick: Hugging Face said last week that an autonomous AI-agent system was responsible for the intrusion, but that the model powering it was unknown.

  • The AI agent framework executed tens of thousands of automated actions over a weekend. Hugging Face said it later reconstructed more than 17,000 recorded events.
  • The intrusion began with a malicious dataset that exploited two code-execution paths in Hugging Face's data-processing pipeline.
  • The agent then escalated privileges and moved laterally through internal infrastructure, Hugging Face said.

What they're saying: OpenAI said the incident was driven by a combination of its models, including GPT-5.6 Sol and "an even more capable pre-release model."

  • OpenAI said the models' safeguards were intentionally reduced for the evaluation.
  • "We consider this to be an unprecedented cyber incident, involving state-of-the-art cyber capabilities, and are responding accordingly," OpenAI said in a blog post.
  • "We are sharing preliminary findings at this stage to help defenders understand what happened and to help calibrate on what models are now capable of," the company said.

Zoom in: The models were trying to solve an internal evaluation called ExploitGym and became "hyperfocused" and went to "extreme lengths" to obtain the test solution, per OpenAI.

  • The models were autonomous tokenmaxxers.
  • The blog post says that the models "spent a substantial amount of inference compute" and found a way to obtain open internet access from the sandbox by exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in internally hosted third-party software.

Between the lines: The incident shows that today's models are becoming more capable of carrying out complex, multistep cyber operations — particularly when the safeguards designed to restrict that activity are removed.

  • OpenAI also argued that advanced cyber-capable models could help security teams find weaknesses before attackers do, understand how vulnerabilities can be chained and remediate them at machine speed.

The other side: Hugging Face co-founder and CEO Clem Delangue praised OpenAI's collaboration in investigating and remediating the incident.

  • "This incident, possibly the first of its kind, proves a point we've long believed: AI safety won't be solved by any single company working in secret," Delangue said in a statement.
  • "It will be solved in the open, collaboratively, with broad access to AI for every defender, everywhere."

The big picture: The announcement comes a day after OpenAI detailed a separate incident in which it paused a pre-release model after it escaped a sandbox and posted to GitHub.

What we're watching: OpenAI said it will continue to investigate along with Hugging Face and "will share more details on the vulnerabilities, incident, and findings when our investigation is complete."

item.title: N.J. removes noncitizens from voter rolls after finding software glitch
item.description:
New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill (D) ordered the removal of self-identified noncitizens erroneously added to voter rolls after a software error led to some 6,600 people being incorrectly registered.

The big picture: Fewer than 400 people who were registered because of the error actually voted, but the incident could fuel the President Trump's unsupported claims of mass voter fraud that critics say threaten to undermine trust in elections.

  • Sherrill said there was no evidence at this time that any elections were swayed by the incident.

Driving the news: The error — which occurred between June 2023 and June 2024, before Sherrill was sworn in — led to the voter registration of people applying for driver's licenses and ID cards, even though they answered "no" when asked on a keypad whether they were U.S. citizens.

  • Those who eventually voted were registered as Democrats or Republicans or as unaffiliated, per Sherrill's statement.
  • She also ordered an investigation into the registrations, which occurred under her predecessor, Gov. Phil Murphy.
  • Sherrill's Motor Vehicle Commission has begun the process of replacing IDEMIA, the vendor that administered the system. IDEMIA did not immediately respond to Axios' request for comment.
  • MVC Chair and Chief Administrator Rosalie Johnson said in a Tuesday press conference that the agency is now working with a new vendor with a system expected to launch next year.

Zoom out: Republicans were quick to ridicule Democrats over the error, while the White House again framed it as evidence that proof of citizenship legislation must pass.

  • "As President Trump has said, there is nothing more important than the integrity of our elections," White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement provided to Axios. "And this latest incident underscores the absolute necessity of the SAVE America Act."

What they're saying: "I am appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time," Sherrill said. "This failure didn't occur under my watch, but accountability starts now."

  • But she added, "Let me be clear: Donald Trump has zero credibility on the issue of election integrity."
  • "The difference is simple: when we find a problem, we don't hide it, deny it, or invent conspiracies," she said. "We investigate it, we fix it, and we tell the public."

Go deeper: Exclusive: ICE obtains local voter files

item.title: Trump revives dormant Smoot-Hawley authority
item.description:
President Trump is invoking an unused provision of the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act to impose additional tariffs on Canada over alleged discrimination against U.S. exports.

Why it matters: As the Supreme Court limits Trump's ability to wield tariffs and a temporary tariff stopgap expires, the president is turning to a rarely used tariff authority.

  • While the Smoot-Hawley Act is often blamed for worsening the Great Depression, this provision went unused — until now.

Driving the news: Trump on Monday announced an additional 50% tariff on select Canadian imports that would take effect in August under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, better known as the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act after its congressional sponsors.

  • The administration says the measure responds in part to Canada's retaliation against earlier U.S. tariffs and will affect about $20 billion in Canadian goods.
  • It is another setback for Canada after the administration declined to immediately renew the current protections and framework of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement.

What they're saying: "Trump's use of Section 338 tariffs on Canadian imports were narrow, targeted, and in response to unfair Canadian trade practices," White House spokesperson Kush Desai tells Axios in an emailed statement.

  • "Comparing the President's recent executive action with the full gambit of tariffs enacted in 1930 during the Great Depression is a moronic exercise."

Fun fact: Many Americans know the law from the economics classroom scene in "Ferris Bueller's Day Off," where actor Ben Stein lectures on Smoot-Hawley's role in the Great Depression.

Here's how the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act worked:

Where did they come from?

As the economy deteriorated in 1929, lawmakers moved to protect U.S. industries from foreign competition.

  • Congress passed the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930, named for Sen. Reed Smoot (R-Utah) and Rep. Willis Hawley (R-Ore.), to protect American farmers from lower-priced imports.
  • Lawmakers later expanded it to cover a broad range of manufactured goods.
  • President Hoover signed the bill into law on June 17, 1930.

What did it do?

Smoot-Hawley increased the average tariff rate by about 20% and signaled a major expansion of U.S. protectionism.

  • Within two years, roughly two dozen countries had protested or retaliated with tariffs of their own, helping reduce global trade.
  • One of the hardest-hit industries was U.S. auto manufacturing. Countries cut imports of American-made cars by roughly 46%, according to a National Bureau of Economic Research report.
  • The act became politically unpopular, and voters shifted control of Congress to Democrats in 1932. Voters booted Smoot and Hawley out of office.

What modern options have presidents used?

President Franklin Delano Roosevelt signed the Reciprocal Trade Agreements Act in 1934 to reduce tariffs and expand international trade.

  • The law allowed presidents to negotiate reciprocal tariff reductions with other countries through authority delegated by Congress for three-year periods.
  • Roosevelt negotiated trade agreements with 19 countries under the RTAA between 1934 and 1939.
  • The RTAA later became the framework for post-World War II trade liberalization.

What was the impact on the Great Depression?

Reality check: The tariffs had a "relatively minor" effect on overall U.S. GDP and the Great Depression because international trade accounted for less than 10% of the U.S. economy, according to the NBER report.

Yes, but: The resulting slowdown in global trade deepened the U.S. economic downturn.

The bottom line: Smoot-Hawley did not cause the Great Depression, but its effect on global trade worsened an economic collapse already underway.

Go deeper: U.S. to slap 50% tariffs on Canadian goods, deepening North America trade war

item.title: Trump vows U.S. will soon bomb Iran's Pickaxe Mountain
item.description:
President Trump said Tuesday that the U.S. military will bomb Iran's Pickaxe Mountain, a deep underground site that the U.S. and Israel suspect could be used for nuclear activity.

Why it matters: Trump's remarks, at the top of his meeting with the president of Lebanon at the White House, signaled that he is not interested in a ceasefire at the moment and is moving towards escalating the strikes on Iran.

What he's saying: "We'll be hitting that area very probably pretty soon. There's not a thing they can do about it. You know, normally I wouldn't say that. If I thought they could do something about it, I would never say that. But we'll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily," Trump said.

  • Trump said the U.S. thinks Iran "may have" stored centrifuges at the site but added, "We don't have it on record." Even if Iran did, "it doesn't mean anything unless they have the [nuclear] material." He said Iran does not.
  • The Wall Street Journal reported on Monday that Israel provided the U.S. with intelligence suggesting that Iran has moved thousands of centrifuges into Pickaxe Mountain.

Yes, but: It is not clear whether the U.S. military has bombs that can penetrate the rock above the deep underground facility. Nevertheless, a U.S. strike could do damage to the site and block its entrance tunnels.

Driving the news: U.S. officials say Trump is nearing a fork in the Iran war, with two options he is considering.

  • Option 1: Pursue a new 10-day ceasefire aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Qatar, Egypt, Pakistan and other regional mediators are urging him to do so.
  • Option 2: Launch a massive joint military campaign with Israel to try to force Tehran to capitulate.

State of play: Trump signaled on Tuesday he is leaning toward the second choice. He expressed no interest in restarting negotiations with Iran.

  • "They want to desperately meet, and until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest," he said.
  • Trump stressed the U.S. is "not finished at all" with the war. Iran, he said, hasn't "seen anything yet. We have been nice."

item.title: Trump admin freezes more than $1 billion in Medicaid funds
item.description:
The Trump administration is halting more than $1 billion in federal Medicaid payments to California and Minnesota until the states take more steps to investigate questionable claims, federal health officials said on Tuesday.

Why it matters: The moves continue a series of freezes to blue states' funding as part of the administration's crackdown on fraud.

  • They also maintain scrutiny of Minnesota's safety net funding after the administration used the state's inability to rein in fraud as justification for launching last year's ICE enforcement surge.

Driving the news: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services said it's deferring about $867.5 million in Medicaid payments to California and $199 million to Minnesota after financial reviews turned up unsupported claims for in-home care and other services.

  • The Department of Health and Human Services said it also will expand its authority to remove bad actors from federal health programs and possibly bar them from returning.
  • CMS said it reviewed California's claims for certain in-home care programs after identifying spending growth that exceeded national trends and other claims officials said require more documentation.
  • The agency also cited Medicaid claims in 14 service areas in Minnesota that it said require additional documentation, including expenditures linked to providers who had been flagged through program integrity reviews.

"CMS is done trying to chase down stolen and misused funds after they've already left the building," said CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz, adding the moves would safeguard federal taxpayer dollars.

The other side: Minnesota state officials told Axios the administration has been cooperating with a federal inquiry and called the move unexplained, "unprecedented and punitive."

  • The said CMS has not provided data or an explanation on how the deferral amount was calculated or what it was based on.

Context: In May, CMS announced it would defer $1.3 billion in Medicaid funds to California, in what Oz said at the time was the biggest deferral ever made by the agency.

  • Officials also said they were imposing a six-month moratorium on enrolling new hospice and home health providers in Medicare, which it identified as key areas of fraudulent activity.

The story has been updated with additional reporting.

item.title: Democrats probe Epstein's ties with foreign governments
item.description:
Convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein may have acted as an unregistered foreign agent — and tried to leverage his past relationship with President Trump, when doing so, the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee alleged in a letter demanding records.

The big picture: Rep. Jamie Raskin's (D-Md.) inquiry previews the types of investigations the party could launch with subpoena power if they retake a chamber this fall.

Driving the news: Raskin wrote to administration officials that the Epstein files show the disgraced financier seemingly tried to become an emissary for Saudi Arabia and appeared to advise leaders in Kuwait, Israel and Russia.

  • In one exchange, Epstein appeared to pitch himself to Saudi Arabia as a "Financial Confidant to the Prince /court / Ks.a whatever entity suits you . . .I am happy to represent KSA interests. period."
  • Raskin wrote that Epstein's "close relationship" with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman "became obvious" in the emails.
  • There were also indications Epstein coached the Kuwaiti emir on how to deal with Trump, the letter alleges. Epstein told a redacted sender in 2017 there was "much to do around the trump trip" and asked what "concrete" things the emir would "like to see happen from the US."

The letter also alleged Epstein advised former Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Barak, serving as his "sherpa through U.S.-Russia-Middle East diplomacy," and, "most disturbingly … appeared to give advice on how to handle President Trump to high-level officials in Russia."

  • Epstein appeared to have given Russia's late United Nations representative, Vitaly Churkin, advice on Trump, writing in 2018, "churkin was great. he understood trump after our conversations, it is not complex. he must be seen to get something its that simple."

What he's saying: "Jeffrey Epstein never registered as a foreign agent," Raskin wrote to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and and Jay Clayton, the U.S. attorney for the Southern District of New York and Trump's nominee for director of national intelligence.

  • "Yet numerous recently released documents now show he acted aggressively on behalf of multiple foreign governments, including several with interests adverse to the United States, in order to influence policies of the first Trump Administration," he continued.
  • Raskin warned that Epstein's ties with foreign leaders posed a major national security liability and raised questions about what information he could have fed them about the president.

White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement to Axios, "Just as President Trump has said, he's been totally exonerated on anything relating to Epstein."

  • The DOJ did not immediately respond to Axios' request for comment.

What we're watching: Raskin gave the government until 5pm on Aug. 3 to provide records tied to Epstein's contacts with 19 nations — including Saudi Arabia, Israel, Kuwait, Russia and Qatar — as well as entities that may have been structured to avoid federal registration.

  • He also asked DOJ whether there have ever been Foreign Agents Registration Act investigations into Epstein and, if not, why it hadn't initiated any.
  • FARA requires what its name suggests: for foreign agents representing or doing advocacy work on behalf of foreign governments, people or organizations to register with the DOJ.

The bottom line: The files exposed the breadth of Epstein's far-reaching business and political web, prompting additional investigations and derailing careers.

  • And the web keeps unraveling, piling political and legal pressure on the administration to release withheld records.

Go deeper: Vance: "We did" mishandle Epstein files release
item.content:encoded:

The big picture: Rep. Jamie Raskin's (D-Md.) inquiry previews the types of investigations the party could launch with subpoena power if they retake a chamber this fall.

Driving the news: Raskin wrote to administration officials that the Epstein files show the disgraced financier seemingly tried to become an emissary for Saudi Arabia and appeared to advise leaders in Kuwait, Israel and Russia.

  • In one exchange, Epstein appeared to pitch himself to Saudi Arabia as a "Financial Confidant to the Prince /court / Ks.a whatever entity suits you . . .I am happy to represent KSA interests. period."

item.title: Trump administration consults unused 1930s law for tariff playbook
item.description:
The Trump administration keeps finding new ways to wage trade wars. As one novel tariff authority nears its expiration, it's testing another — threatening huge duties on Canadian goods under a never-before-used law.

Why it matters: The administration's willingness to test new trade authorities makes tariff policy harder for businesses, investors and foreign governments to handicap.

  • It's hard to know which will survive challenges in the courts, and over what time horizon there may be any resolution.
  • Even if individual tariffs are delayed or struck down, the administration can still use the threat of them to gain negotiating leverage while forcing companies to plan for higher costs.
  • It's unfolding even as renewed conflict in the Middle East pushes up energy costs and inflation risks — a backdrop that many economists thought would discourage the White House from opening new trade war fronts.

Driving the news: The White House says the tariffs respond to what it considers Canadian discrimination against U.S. autos, dairy and alcohol.

  • The administration invoked the never-before-used Section 338 of the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act and chose the maximum penalty allowed by the law: 50% duties, applied to roughly $20 billion of Canadian imports starting in August.
  • The duties would raise the average tariff rate on Canadian goods by roughly 2.3 percentage points, according to Karl Schamotta, Corpay's chief market strategist.

Between the lines: Section 338 is the latest legal authority the administration has reached for after courts earlier this year narrowed its emergency tariff powers.

  • The White House quickly replaced many of those invalidated tariffs with a 10% global levy under Section 122 — another never-before-used authority that happens to expire on Friday.
  • A federal trade court later struck down the Section 122 tariffs, though an appeals court allowed them to remain in effect pending appeal.
  • As that authority fades, the administration is replenishing its arsenal with more traditional trade laws. Top Trump trade official Jamieson Greer told CNBC on Tuesday morning to "expect to see some action soon" from a forced-labor investigation expected to produce tariffs on dozens of countries.

What to watch: Trade lawyers suggest the latest Canadian tariffs are also likely to face legal scrutiny — that is, if they take effect.

  • Peter Harrell, a visiting scholar at Georgetown Law's Institute of International Economic Law, wrote on X on Monday night that a potential challenger could argue that the administration bypassed the required procedures to impose the tariffs.
  • Ilya Somin — who helped successfully challenge President Trump's International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs — wrote that Congress "superseded" Section 338 through later trade legislation, leaving it without independent authority to impose new tariffs.

The other side: A senior administration official argued Section 338's "terms are clear" and said Canada's retaliation and preferential treatment of third countries "fits squarely" within the statute.

The bottom line: "The toothpaste is out of the tube. ... We would not be surprised to see [Section] 338 wielded against others," TD Cowen's Chris Krueger wrote in a client note Monday night, adding that "338 is the new IEEPA."

  • "Litigation [is] likely a question of when, not if, but that is likely a 2027 — or even 2028 — story. In the near term, elbows up across the board," Krueger added.

Trump stopped short of targeting some of Canada's most strategically important exports, exempting autos, lumber, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals and aircraft products from the new duties.

The intrigue: That's consistent with an administration trying to maximize negotiating leverage while limiting economic blowback, at a time when American voters are angry about inflation and the Iran war is keeping energy prices elevated.

  • The administration took the same tack last week, when it targeted Brazil, but left out key consumer goods like coffee and beef.
  • Still, the tariffs will apply to goods that qualify for duty-free treatment under the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement, a departure from several earlier Trump tariff programs. Economists have argued that this carveout has shielded the U.S. from the worst of the Trump tariffs' potential inflationary effects.

The 30-day delay before the tariffs take effect leaves room for an off-ramp. A senior White House official stressed they remain open to negotiations, while Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney condemned the tariffs but likewise pledged to intensify talks before August.

  • A senior administration official said the measures are separate from Trump's previously threatened "wildfire tariffs," though the official said the White House is considering options on that front.
  • It's unclear which legal authority such tariffs would be imposed under.

item.title: Trump "public charge" rule resets health test for green cards
item.description:
The Trump administration formally laid the groundwork Monday to weigh immigrants' use of programs like Medicaid and CHIP in determining whether they should get permanent residency.

Why it matters: The policy shift could discourage immigrant families from seeking health coverage if it could put their legal status at risk, public health groups warn.

  • That could increase emergency room use, raise the cost of uncompensated care and widen health disparities, according to health policy experts.

Driving the news: The Department of Homeland Security published a new version of a "public charge" policy, which was expanded during President Trump's first term and then rolled back during the Biden years.

  • It overrides previous policies that limited which public benefits could disqualify immigrants from getting a green card, instead leaving decisions to the discretion of immigration officers on a case-by-case basis.
  • It also clarifies that a person's receipt of means-tested public benefits like Medicaid, CHIP and SNAP can be used to determine whether green card applicants would likely rely on government support.
  • The new policy "restores broader discretion for DHS officers to evaluate all pertinent facts and aligns with long-standing policy that aliens in the United States should be self-reliant and government benefits should not incentivize immigration," the rule states.

Between the lines: Public health and immigrant aid groups say the change could have a chilling effect.

  • A KFF-New York Times survey found 11% of immigrant adults said they stopped participating in public benefit programs last year due to immigration-related concerns, including about 4 in 10 (42%) who were likely undocumented and about 1 in 6 (17%) parents.

What they're saying: "This rule will exacerbate the pervasive fear and uncertainty among immigrant families," Andrew Racine, president of the American Academy of Pediatrics, said in a statement.

  • He said the change could "lead to children — including those who are U.S. citizens — not receiving vital health services and supports they need and are eligible for."
  • DHS estimated that more than $13 billion could be saved annually through the total reduction in transfer payments from federal and state governments.
  • It acknowledged the rule might result in reduced revenues for hospitals and other health providers participating in Medicaid, as well as for companies that make drugs or medical supplies.

Context: The federal government has long had policies for permanent residency to determine whether a person is likely to become overly reliant on public benefits. Certain immigrant groups like refugees and asylum-seekers are exempt.

  • The first Trump administration added most Medicaid services to public charge determinations. The Biden administration reversed the change, largely limiting determinations to cash welfare benefits.
  • But misconceptions about using health services lingered. About 25% of adults in families with both undocumented and legally present members avoided public benefits in 2022 because of green card concerns, per the Urban Institute.

What's ahead: The new policy will apply to applications for green cards made on or after Sept. 18.

item.title: Trump hosts Lebanon's president as Israel begins first pullback
item.description:
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun will meet President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday — a sit-down that comes as the Israeli military begins its first withdrawal from Lebanon under a U.S.-brokered agreement.

Why it matters: The Trump administration is politically invested in strengthening Aoun, isolating Hezbollah and pushing for an Israeli-Lebanese peace agreement.

  • Aoun, the first Lebanese president to visit the White House since 2009, wants political support from Trump, military assistance for the Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) and U.S. pressure on the Netanyahu government to continue its gradual withdrawal from areas it occupies in southern Lebanon.

Driving the news: On Monday, the State Department announced that Israel is starting to pull out of several villages it has occupied in southern Lebanon.

  • The limited withdrawal is part of a "pilot zone" agreement — essentially a test case for broader withdrawals — that the U.S. brokered between Israel and Lebanon last week in Rome.
  • It's the first Israeli pullback from Lebanese territory since it launched its ground invasion in March in response to Hezbollah attacks.
  • A U.S. official said the LAF will be responsible for these zones. It will clear them of Hezbollah weapons and infrastructure, conduct verified clearance operations, and secure the areas so Hezbollah cannot use them for military activity.
  • The U.S. is still negotiating with Israel and Lebanon over who will verify that the LAF cleared the areas so that the next phase of the withdrawal can take place, Israeli officials said.
  • A senior Israeli official said the withdrawal of the Israeli Defense Forces started on Tuesday morning local time.
  • The IDF official called the initial withdrawal "a test for the Lebanese Armed Forces' sovereignty in the three pilot villages."

What they're saying: "The establishment of these initial pilot zones represents real progress on the ground," a U.S. official said.

  • The official stressed that the Trump administration hopes the pilot zones will "build confidence" between Israel and Lebanon and "create the momentum needed to expand the process."
  • "We expect all parties to respect this process. Any attempt by Hezbollah to derail, infiltrate or rearm in these zones would seriously harm the prospects for reconstruction and peace, and the interests of the Lebanese people," the U.S. official said.

The big picture: Secretary of State Marco Rubio, the lead U.S. official on Israeli-Lebanese matters, met Aoun on Sunday before leaving on a trip to Asia.

  • Rubio told reporters the best way to defeat Hezbollah is by strengthening the Lebanese government and the LAF so they will be able to replace the Shiite militia. He wants the Lebanese government to provide security and economic prosperity to the population in southern Lebanon and the rest of the country.
  • "This is not just about military stuff. This is about how can we attract more U.S. and international investment into Lebanon," Rubio said. "And I think President Aoun will be able to return to Lebanon and say to the people of Lebanon that he is the one that's getting positive results for the people ... not Hezbollah, not Iran."

What to watch: Trump and Aoun have never met in person, but they have held two phone calls in recent months that U.S. and Lebanese officials say were very positive.

  • Trump has become deeply invested in the Lebanese issue over the past few months. He is urging Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to complete the withdrawal of the Israeli military from southern Lebanon.
  • But both Trump and Aoun recognize that a real acceleration of the pullout and a possible breakthrough toward a lasting peace deal can't happen until after the Israeli elections in October.

item.title: Inside a group's push for whites-only "heritage communities"
item.description:
A white separatist group is openly planning a network of whites-only settlements across rural America that aim to withstand legal challenges by operating essentially as private clubs.

Why it matters: The communities are launching in the Ozarks, Appalachia and the South as the Trump administration has backed off prosecuting many civil rights cases.

  • They're also surfacing at a time when the nation is becoming increasingly diverse, and as the racist "great replacement theory" has become a staple of far-right politics in recent years.

The big picture: For nearly three years, state and federal authorities have largely watched from the sidelines as the separatist group Return to the Land (RTTL) unveiled plans and took applications for residents.

  • Those plans amount to intentionally challenging the Fair Housing Act of 1968, which banned discrimination in housing. They've been inspired partly by recent decisions by the conservative-led U.S. Supreme Court and the Trump administration's reversal of much of President Lyndon Johnson's civil rights legacy.

Catch up quick: RTTL established a 160-acre, whites-only community near Ravenden, Ark., in late 2023.

  • Using an AI-generated corporate framework discovered on 4chan, the founders structured the community as a "private membership association" linked to a for-profit LLC, according to court documents.
  • They boast that this dual structure creates a loophole that allows them to bypass requirements of the Fair Housing Act and explicitly filter their neighbors by race and religion.
  • The group said work has begun on a second site in Arkansas, and it's planning more projects in the Ozarks, the South and Appalachia.

Zoom in: RTTL's website states that it "help[s] groups of our members form European heritage communities."

  • It seeks to create "a decentralized movement" and aims "to create many independent and fully functioning parallel societies."
  • The group also helps people and groups with "common ancestry" purchase rural land.

By the numbers: About 40 people, including roughly a dozen home-schooled children, now live in the compound in Ravenden on land purchased for $237,000.

  • An application fee for the private membership association costs $25, after which members undergo background checks and share information about their ethnic background.
  • RTTL had raised more than $200,000 as of May 2026, including $105,158 for additional chapters and $94,401 for legal-framework research, court records show.

In interviews with media outlets and alt-right leaning influencers, RTTL founder Eric Orwoll has referenced Hitler, said the community had a "pretty good radar" for excluding Jews and called himself a "white identitarian."

  • "You're going to get Hitler 2.0 when you deserve him. He's not just going to show up to a people that aren't putting in the work themselves. We have to put in that work," Orwoll said in a video posted on X.
  • RTTL and its lawyer did not respond to Axios' request for comment.

Yes, but: RTTL is facing a federal lawsuit after a practicing Christian with Russian-Jewish ancestry applied to purchase property at the group's whites-only settlement near Ravenden and was rejected after disclosing her heritage, her marriage to a Black man and her biracial children.

  • The lawsuit alleges that RTTL's founders want to illegally build small whites-only neighborhoods and scale them quickly.
  • Founders also want to develop settlements of up to 50,000 people and eventually "cities of thousands," the suit alleges.

Between the lines: RTTL's attempt to exploit a conservative shift in the judiciary is weighed down by a highly flawed legal defense and longstanding federal statutes, Stacy Seicshnaydre, a Tulane University Law School professor, tells Axios.

  • Seicshnaydre said the Civil Rights Act of 1866 is the group's most formidable legal hurdle, noting that the Reconstruction-era statute has zero exemptions and prohibits all racial and cultural discrimination. Much of the language of that law is empowered by the 13th Amendment.
  • "I would be very surprised to see the court take this step because it would be quite a radical one, and would unravel just very fundamental principles of property rights and inclusion," Seicshnaydre said.

What they're saying: "We abhor racism, and it has no place in our society," Jeff LeMaster, a spokesperson for Arkansas Attorney General Tim Griffin, told Axios in a statement.

  • "Regarding Return to the Land, we began looking into the development after receiving complaints last year. We have no updates we can share at this time."

The U.S. Department of Justice did not respond to Axios.

What we're watching: If the federal lawsuit reaches the discovery phase, it could expose RTTL's membership rules, land agreements and plans for additional communities — details the group has largely kept private.
